label: Console and You
title: The Console and You
lead: ...and the many commands for the few~. Register development commands that can be ran via the game's console.
bodyHtml:
How the game console works

Vanilla ConsoleScript stores commands in a static ConsoleScript.Commands list. Each Command owns a name, description, action, optional autofill, and optional argument descriptions.

CUCoreLib's ConsoleCommandRegistry adds commands after vanilla command registration, so dependent mods do not need their own console Harmony patch.

ConsoleCommandRegistry.Register(
    string name,
    string description,
    Command.Action action,
    Dictionary<int, List<string>> argAutofill = null,
    params (string shortDesc, string longDesc)[] argDescription
);
Argument Type What it needs
namestringThe command text the player types, without spaces. Example: whereami.
descriptionstringHelp text shown by the console command list.
actionCommand.ActionThe delegate that runs when the command is executed. It receives string[] args.
argAutofillDictionary<int, List<string>>Optional autocomplete suggestions per argument index. Index 0 means the first argument after the command name.
argDescriptionparams (string, string)[]Optional argument labels and descriptions. The first string is the short label shown in command usage; the second string explains it.

Inside the action, args[0] is the command name. The first value the player typed after the command is args[1].

When to use a command

Use console commands for debugging, development tools, diagnostics, and explicit player/admin actions. Do not hide core gameplay behavior behind a console command if it should happen automatically in normal play.

Descriptions and argument metadata matter: the vanilla console shows them while typing, and the first argument is args[1] because args[0] is the command name.

Autofill and argument descriptions

The game's Command constructor auto-adds bool autofill for argument descriptions beginning with bool, and position autofill for descriptions beginning with position.

ConsoleCommandRegistry.Register(
    "teleportmarker",
    "Teleports the player to a saved marker.",
    args =>
    {
        CUCoreUtils.ConsoleCheckForWorld(ConsoleScript.instance);
        string marker = args[1];
        bool snapToGround = bool.Parse(args[2]);
    },
    new Dictionary<int, List<string>>
    {
        // Suggestions for args[1].
        { 0, new List<string> { "home", "camp", "exit" } }
    },
    ("string marker", "Saved marker name"),
    ("bool snapToGround", "Whether to place the player on the ground")
);

Because the second argument description starts with bool, vanilla adds true and false suggestions automatically. Descriptions beginning with position get cursor, player, random, and #.#.
bodyText: How the game console works Vanilla ConsoleScript stores commands in a static ConsoleScript.Commands list. Each Command owns a name, description, action, optional autofill, and optional argument descriptions. CUCoreLib's ConsoleCommandRegistry adds commands after vanilla command registration, so dependent mods do not need their own console Harmony patch. ConsoleCommandRegistry.Register( string name, string description, Command.Action action, Dictionary<int, List<string>> argAutofill = null, params (string shortDesc, string longDesc)[] argDescription ); Argument Type What it needs name string The command text the player types, without spaces. Example: whereami . description string Help text shown by the console command list. action Command.Action The delegate that runs when the command is executed. It receives string[] args . argAutofill Dictionary<int, List<string>> Optional autocomplete suggestions per argument index. Index 0 means the first argument after the command name. argDescription params (string, string)[] Optional argument labels and descriptions. The first string is the short label shown in command usage; the second string explains it. Inside the action, args[0] is the command name. The first value the player typed after the command is args[1] . When to use a command Use console commands for debugging, development tools, diagnostics, and explicit player/admin actions. Do not hide core gameplay behavior behind a console command if it should happen automatically in normal play. Descriptions and argument metadata matter: the vanilla console shows them while typing, and the first argument is args[1] because args[0] is the command name. Autofill and argument descriptions The game's Command constructor auto-adds bool autofill for argument descriptions beginning with bool , and position autofill for descriptions beginning with position . ConsoleCommandRegistry.Register( "teleportmarker", "Teleports the player to a saved marker.", args => { CUCoreUtils.ConsoleCheckForWorld(ConsoleScript.instance); string marker = args[1]; bool snapToGround = bool.Parse(args[2]); }, new Dictionary<int, List<string>> { // Suggestions for args[1]. { 0, new List<string> { "home", "camp", "exit" } } }, ("string marker", "Saved marker name"), ("bool snapToGround", "Whether to place the player on the ground") ); Because the second argument description starts with bool , vanilla adds true and false suggestions automatically. Descriptions beginning with position get cursor , player , random , and #.# .
